talk_title: Distinguishing selection in normal tissues from cancer causation

speaker.name: Kamila Axelrod
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Department of Genetics

MOA_summary: Axelrod presented a quantitative framework that compares mutation frequencies in cancers (Y) to frequencies in matched normal tissues (X) to compute a carcinogenic effect (odds ratio). Using esophageal SCC, AML and other cancers, she showed that some canonical drivers (e.g., TP53) have high carcinogenic effects, whereas others (e.g., NOTCH1, PIK3CA) appear neutral or even cancer‑inhibiting, indicating that positive selection in normal tissues can distort perceived driver status.
key_findings: Carcinogenic effect is calculated as the odds ratio of cancer vs. normal tissue mutation frequencies.
TP53 shows a carcinogenic effect in the thousands in esophageal SCC, confirming strong driver status.
NOTCH1 mutations are more frequent in normal esophagus than in cancer, suggesting a tumor‑inhibiting role.
PIK3CA, FAD1, EP300 show low carcinogenic effects, indicating limited contribution to malignancy.
In AML, classic drivers (e.g., FLT3) have high carcinogenic effects, while TET2/DNMT3A have low effects.
open_questions: Can age‑dependent mutation frequency be used prospectively to identify high‑risk individuals?
How do environmental exposures alter the normal‑tissue mutation frequencies used in the model?

talk_title: Clonal evolution and promotion in mouse skin carcinogenesis

speaker.name: Alan Balmain
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Helen Diller Family Comprehensive Cancer Center

models.name: DMBA/TPA skin carcinogenesis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild‑type, HRAS‑/‑ knockout, various spontaneous RAS mutations

clinical_data.value: few tumors, but 30% KRAS G13R in benign, higher in malignant

MOA_summary: Balmain demonstrated that initiating mutations (e.g., DMBA‑induced HRAS Q61L) persist as latent clones for months, but tumor formation requires a promoting inflammatory stimulus (TPA). Promotion drives clonal expansion of specific RAS mutants, with immune cell infiltration (neutrophils, monocytes) being essential; HRAS and KRAS mutants compete for clonal dominance, and HRAS loss reveals KRAS‑driven tumors.
key_findings: A single DMBA dose creates ~30,000–50,000 mutations, including HRAS Q61L, which remains latent for up to a year.
TPA promotion induces massive clonal expansion of HRAS Q61L, accounting for >90% of tumors.
Spontaneous KRAS G12D/G13R clones expand with TPA but disappear unless HRAS is knocked out.
HRAS knockout mice develop KRAS‑driven tumors, revealing competitive clonal interactions.
Promotion requires innate immune cells; NSG mice (immune‑deficient) develop few tumors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HRAS and KRAS mutants show reciprocal competition; removal of HRAS permits KRAS‑driven tumorigenesis.

combination_strategies.rationale: Induces inflammation and hyperplasia to promote expansion of initiated clones
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor formation with HRAS Q61L

combination_strategies.rationale: Tests whether inflammation without specific immune recruitment drives promotion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Induces inflammation but minimal clonal expansion

open_questions: What precise molecular signals from neutrophils/monocytes drive HRAS‑clone expansion?
How do different genetic backgrounds modulate promotion sensitivity?

talk_title: Germline‑somatic associations in cancer using real‑world panel sequencing

speaker.name: Jian Carrot Zhang
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Computational Oncology Service

biomarkers.name: MDM4 germline variant (1q32 locus)
biomarkers.finding: Associated with increased TP53 mutation frequency across cancers (p<1e‑6)

MOA_summary: Zhang described a pipeline that imputes genome‑wide germline genotypes from off‑target reads of clinical tumor panels, enabling GWAS of germline variants influencing somatic driver acquisition. A germline locus near MDM4 was linked to higher TP53 mutation rates, likely via increased MDM4 expression reducing p53 activity, and this association persisted after adjusting for mutational burden and smoking.
key_findings: Off‑target reads from panel sequencing can accurately impute common germline variants (R²>0.8).
Pan‑cancer GWAS identified a 1q32/M​DM4 locus associated with TP53 mutations; replicated in Dana‑Farber and TCGA cohorts.
CRISPR deletion of the implicated enhancer reduced MDM4 expression, confirming functional relevance.
The MDM4 risk allele is enriched in stage IV disease and lung metastasis in breast cancer, independent of TP53 status.
Ancestry‑specific germline variant explains part of the higher ERG fusion frequency in European‑ancestry prostate cancer.
open_questions: How many additional germline‑somatic associations remain undiscovered at larger sample sizes?
Can germline‑driven selection be leveraged for preventive interventions?

talk_title: Cancer driver mutations in normal tissues and their relation to cancer risk

speaker.name: Nuria López‑Bigas
speaker.affiliation: Institute for Research in Biomedicine, Barcelona

models.name: whole‑genome sequencing of normal colon crypts, lung stem cells, blood cells
normal bladder cytobrush duplex sequencing (45 donors)
models.genotype_or_key_features: male vs female, smoker vs non‑smoker

MOA_summary: López‑Bigas introduced MutRisk, a model that integrates tissue‑specific mutation rates, trinucleotide signatures, and chromatin context to estimate per‑site mutation probabilities in normal stem cells. Multiplying by stem‑cell pool sizes yields estimates of how many cells harbor specific driver mutations (e.g., ~1.5 million TP53‑mutant lung basal cells in a smoker). The analysis shows that random mutagenesis alone cannot explain observed cancer incidence, highlighting the need for clonal selection driven by promotion and microenvironmental factors.
key_findings: MutRisk predicts highly unequal per‑site mutation probabilities; some TP53 codons are orders of magnitude more likely to mutate.
Estimated numbers of TP53‑mutant stem cells range from ~18 000 in colon to ~1.5 million in smoker lung tissue.
Combinations of driver mutations (e.g., APC + APC + KRAS) are exceedingly rare without clonal selection (<1 per million individuals).
Duplex sequencing of normal bladder reveals strong positive selection for RBM10 truncations and TP53 mutations, with higher DN/DS in males.
TERT promoter mutations show strong selection only in smokers, linking exposure to clonal expansion.
open_questions: How do specific environmental exposures reshape the selection landscape in different tissues?
Can MutRisk be integrated with longitudinal sampling to predict individual cancer risk?

cited_papers: López‑Bigas et al. 2024 Nat Rev Cancer
